metadata.from: Bob (Robert E. Hanifen) — confirmed by return address on envelope: PFC Robert E. Hanifen, and signed 'Bobby' at end
metadata.to: James C. Hanifen (father, James Edward Hanifen Sr.) — addressed 'Dear Dad'
metadata.location_written_from: Naples, Italy — arrived there the 20th, writing from Red Cross building
metadata.postmark: U.S. Army Postal Service, FEB 23, 37 [?] — likely FEB 23 1946
metadata.envelope_notes: Return address: PFC Robert E. Hanifen - 17170600, Sqd. A3 - APO 11898-A, Postmaster New York, N.Y. Marked 'Free' (military free postage). Addressed to Mr. James C. Hanifen, 904 Keo Way, Des Moines, Iowa.
metadata.mood_tone: Energetic, observational, candid, and at times crude — Bob is vividly describing his first impressions of Naples and North Africa, with frank and unfiltered commentary on the local population, war damage, and daily life as a soldier
metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is fast and informal with many ligatures and abbreviations. Some words are difficult to parse — especially proper nouns and slang. The letter is written on multiple pages of American Red Cross stationery. Several passages with profanity or crude remarks are legible in context. The diagram on page 2 (a small sketch of barracks layout) is noted.
metadata.historical_context: WWII aftermath — Bob has arrived in Naples, Italy, likely as part of post-war occupation or transition forces. The city shows heavy bomb damage from wartime Allied operations. He references the Air Corps and Infantry having 'worked this place over.' He passed through Casablanca (Morocco) en route. References to 'Degas' (likely Italians, slang) and wartime conditions. The letter is dated February 1946, just months after the end of WWII.

pages.transcription:
Feb. 21, 46

Dear Dad,

Arrived in Naples the 20th at 1200 eat chow on ship and left at 1300 the 7 Service Depot. We came up in trucks 50 to a trucks. Big open air job. Right through the heart of the city. Those Air Corps and Infantry men really worked this place over. Blew hell out of it. Most of it is cleaned up now. It wasn't hurt as bad as the papers in the states said so. The Degas [Dagos?] took and thumbed their noses [at the] Dolan [?] sign and smiled all at the same time. What filthy bastards. One girl sounded off and said look at the American son-of-a-bitch. Swear[s]

a hard time. We pulled into a big area filled up with big buildings, palm trees, swimming pools, tennis courts, roller skating rinks, stadium and really a beautiful place. Statues all over. What was funny tho was all the holes in the buildings. It was formerly a German Headquarters. It was Mussolini's Naples Expedition [Expeditionary?] way — round, supposed to represent Africa. It is really nice. We live in small wooded barracks with a big building in the Patio [sketch of barracks layout] as nice barracks deal. Good food. PWs do all the work on the field. All we do is pulled guard. Pretty rough for a lot of Italians has been going in camp by 19 and 20 [hundred hours]. All I think [know?] about to tell they say. Sound like

war at night around here. Am in the Red Cross building now. Beautiful. Wish I had a camera to show it to you in picture. It took us 10 days to get here. We stopped a day at Casablanca. What a town. Filthy Arabs. Beg for shit damn near. We food away from the sea hull [?] if they can. I at least [?] in better shape than Naples but it really took a pounding. 3 mph we are lying in shallow water holes in them like your [?]. Big liners ran [?] Mercury [?] and big lined [?] aground our to the left of them. One boat is sunk so only the mast is still showing. Those Arabs are really the deal

The native cops beat hell out of them with the back [of] jack [?]. All the time for getting near the boat. They say in Naples here, they will sell you for anything. Had a [robbery?] last month. Beat hell and [someone?] and stole everything but his shorts. If you get a pass to town you are allowed to carry your own choice of weapon. .45 Carbine, M1 or whatever you get your hands on. This American Dego [Dago] are in like Flynn over here. All of them no Dego. The women are slut[s] all of them. As one time for two cigarettes. Cigarettes all for 2325 lire a carton [equals] $2 dollars in American money to anyone. Down town 36 or 40

bucks. But that is black market. I can send home all my money, which is mid [?] over the Army pay table. Not will be with my insurance and one band [?] out about 46 bucks. You can live damn good on your cigarettes ration. Just got back from the show. Doll Face and the Spanish Main. Beautiful theater, about the size of the Shrine [theater] here. Huge place. I will enclose this map of our voyage till we probably ship in 48 hrs. from now. A lot left of barracks already. Well Dad goodbye for now.

Your loving son Bobby

full_transcription:
Feb. 21, 46

Dear Dad,

Arrived in Naples the 20th at 1200 eat chow on ship and left at 1300 the 7 Service Depot. We came up in trucks 50 to a truck. Big open air job. Right through the heart of the city. Those Air Corps and Infantry men really worked this place over. Blew hell out of it. Most of it is cleaned up now. It wasn't hurt as bad as the papers in the states said so. The Degas [Dagos?] took and thumbed their noses [at the] [?] sign and smiled all at the same time. What filthy bastards. One girl sounded off and said look at the American son-of-a-bitch. Swear[s] a hard time. We pulled into a big area filled up with big buildings, palm trees, swimming pools, tennis courts, roller skating rinks, stadium and really a beautiful place. Statues all over. What was funny tho was all the holes in the buildings. It was formerly a German Headquarters. It was Mussolini's Naples Expedition [Expeditionary?] way — round, supposed to represent Africa. It is really nice. We live in small wooded barracks with a big building in the Patio [sketch of barracks layout] as nice barracks deal. Good food. PWs do all the work on the field. All we do is pulled guard. Pretty rough for a lot of Italians has been going in camp by 19 and 20 [hundred hours]. All I think [know?] about to tell they say. Sound like war at night around here. Am in the Red Cross building now. Beautiful. Wish I had a camera to show it to you in picture. It took us 10 days to get here. We stopped a day at Casablanca. What a town. Filthy Arabs. Beg for shit damn near. We food away from the sea hull [?] if they can. At least in better shape than Naples but it really took a pounding. 3 mph [?] we are lying in shallow water holes in them like your [?]. Big liners ran aground to the left of them. One boat is sunk so only the mast is still showing. Those Arabs are really the deal — the native cops beat hell out of them with the back [of] jack [?]. All the time for getting near the boat. They say in Naples here, they will sell you for anything. Had a [robbery?] last month. Beat hell and stole everything but his shorts. If you get a pass to town you are allowed to carry your own choice of weapon. .45 Carbine, M1 or whatever you get your hands on. This American Dego [Dago] are in like Flynn over here. All of them no Dego. The women are sluts all of them. As one time for two cigarettes. Cigarettes all for 2325 lire a carton [equals] $2 dollars in American money to anyone. Down town 36 or 40 bucks. But that is black market. I can send home all my money, which is mid [?] over the Army pay table. Not will be with my insurance and one band [?] out about 46 bucks. You can live damn good on your cigarettes ration. Just got back from the show. Doll Face and the Spanish Main. Beautiful theater, about the size of the Shrine [theater] here. Huge place. I will enclose this map of our voyage till we probably ship in 48 hrs. from now. A lot left of barracks already. Well Dad goodbye for now.

Your loving son Bobby
summary: Written February 21, 1946, PFC Robert 'Bobby' Hanifen writes to his father from Naples, Italy, describing his arrival and first impressions of the war-damaged city. He vividly recounts the journey through a bombed-out Naples, his barracks (a former German/Mussolini headquarters), a stopover in Casablanca, and observations of the local Italian and Arab populations — often in crude and unfiltered terms typical of soldier correspondence. He also discusses the black market cigarette trade, military pay, and having just seen two movies at a beautiful local theater, with a promise to enclose a map of his sea voyage.
